---
title: ""
pagetitle: "Shreyas Meher | Postdoctoral Researcher"
page-layout: full
toc: false
---

::: {.home-hero}
::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-8}
[Postdoctoral Researcher · Erasmus University Rotterdam]{.eyebrow}

# Shreyas Meher {.hero-name}

[I study how governments control information online, and how democratic institutions erode.]{.hero-lede}

As a postdoctoral researcher at Erasmus University Rotterdam, I pursue these questions on the [TWIN4DEM project](https://twin4dem.eu/) with [Clara Egger](https://claramarieegger.wordpress.com/) and [Asya Zhelyazkova](https://asyazhelyazkovasite.wordpress.com/), where we build machine learning frameworks that model democratic processes and stress-test institutional resilience, including digital twins of executive aggrandizement in parliamentary democracies.

My toolkit is computational: fine-tuned language models, text-as-data pipelines, and causal research designs. My work has appeared in *Political Analysis*, *Research & Politics*, and *Behavioral Sciences*, and my NLP models for political conflict were developed through the NSF-funded [ConfliBERT project](https://eventdata.utdallas.edu/) with [Patrick T. Brandt](https://www.utdallas.edu/~pbrandt/). I earned my Ph.D. in Public Policy & Political Economy from the University of Texas at Dallas in 2025.

[Digital Governance]{.interest-pill}
[Computational Social Science]{.interest-pill}
[NLP & LLMs]{.interest-pill}
[Democratic Backsliding]{.interest-pill}
[Reinforcement Learning]{.interest-pill}

::: {.hero-actions}
[Explore my research](research.qmd){.btn-accent}
[Publications](publications.qmd){.btn-ghost}
[CV (PDF)](CV.pdf){.btn-ghost}
:::
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
![](headshot.jpg){#img-me fig-alt="Portrait of Shreyas Meher"}

::: {.hero-contact}
[meher@essb.eur.nl](mailto:meher@essb.eur.nl)<br>
Rotterdam, the Netherlands
:::
:::
:::
:::

## What I work on {.home-section}

```{=html}
<div class="grid">
  <div class="g-col-12 g-col-md-4">
    <a href="research.html" class="pillar-card pillar-dg">
      <h3><span class="pillar-dot"></span>Digital Governance</h3>
      <p>Who censors, and why? I study government content takedowns in democracies, internet filtering in autocracies, and the political economy of digital sovereignty.</p>
    </a>
  </div>
  <div class="g-col-12 g-col-md-4">
    <a href="research.html" class="pillar-card pillar-css">
      <h3><span class="pillar-dot"></span>Computational Social Science</h3>
      <p>I build specialized language models, such as ConfliBERT and ConflLlama, that turn unstructured political text into structured data for conflict research.</p>
    </a>
  </div>
  <div class="g-col-12 g-col-md-4">
    <a href="research.html" class="pillar-card pillar-ipe">
      <h3><span class="pillar-dot"></span>Democratic Backsliding</h3>
      <p>How do executives expand power inside democratic frameworks? I study legislative oversight, executive aggrandizement, and institutional resilience.</p>
    </a>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Selected work {.home-section}

::: {.pub-entry}
[Extractive versus Generative Language Models for Political Conflict Text Classification]{.pub-title}<br>
[*Political Analysis*, 2025 · with Brandt, Alsarra, D'Orazio, Heintze, Khan, Osorio & Sianan]{.pub-venue}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[ConflLlama: Domain-Specific Adaptation of Large Language Models for Conflict Event Classification]{.pub-title}<br>
[*Research & Politics*, 2025 · with Patrick T. Brandt]{.pub-venue}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[Building Capacity or Building Checks? What EU Enlargement Reports Emphasise and Democratic Backsliding]{.pub-title}<br>
[Working paper, EPSS 2026 (Belfast) · with Asya Zhelyazkova & Clara Egger]{.pub-venue}
:::

[All publications →](publications.qmd){.more-link}
